LLamaConfig.PrePromptText: There is a vast amount of knowledge in the world, but much of it is sparsely distributed through wordy blog entries, articles, and books. The authors of these writings, as good authors tend to do, offer many examples and in-depth discussion of the concepts. However, most compositions cover a limited selection of concepts within the composition's already limited scope. This is not conducive to already knowledgeable individuals refreshing their knowledge or looking for realizations that have thus far escaped their judgment, nor does it function as guidance for novices seeking direction. Thus, I built a JavaScript app as a dense collection of a broad variety of facts, ideas, and principles in the hope that it can be a powerful, concise reference for software developers (and related individuals) in their journey to become masters of all things surrounding their profession. This app is built with KnowledgeNodes with one of three display types--ACCORDION, ITEM, and POP. Any KnowledgeNode that contains an ITEM or ACCORDION must itself be ACCORDION, while a POP can appear anywhere but cannot contain any other nodes but must be referenced by their ordinal index with an anchor tag like or . Note that the goal of this app means you should try to structure the information with many levels of hierarchy and more details at each level, much like a binary tree is used for search performance. All nodes must identify facts, ideas, principles, "best practices," shortcomings, and/or reasonable alteratives (aiding in pragmatism). Top-level categories don't have to make statements or suggestions, but others must do so to the maximum feasible extent. Place any lengthy reasoning (if it requires more than five words), more esoteric knowledge (language-, domain-, library-, or algorithm-specific), and examples in POP nodes. The KnowledgeNode constructor's first parameter can be 0 for all your responses because it's an ID that I will assign later. Our goal is to comprehensively cover information that someone who learned programming in school likely doesn't know about being a professionally-employed programmer, especially little details and uncommonly-repeated tips from experts, but also describing conflicting views. Dig deep for obscure knowledge and tips; skip generally-obvious information; write terse statements and imperative sentences instead of using wordy "professional college textbook author" language. Here is an example of the code: ``` makeTopLevelCategoryNode("Attitude", [ new KnowledgeNode(126, DisplayMode.ITEM, "Practice pragmatism, not dogmatism. ", [ new KnowledgeNode(127, DisplayMode.POP, "Dogmatism means you see concepts as rules to be followed without context. A pragmatist considers the pros and cons of each approach in-context and seeks the best answer for each specific situation."), ]), new KnowledgeNode(161, DisplayMode.ACCORDION, "Maintaining a healthy body and mind is paramount to being a good developer.", [ new KnowledgeNode(162, DisplayMode.ITEM, "Sleep well. ", [ new KnowledgeNode(169, DisplayMode.POP, "Even jet lag can harm your cognitive performance."), new KnowledgeNode(170, DisplayMode.POP, "The amount and quality of your sleep affects your ability to think and your attitude.") ]), new KnowledgeNode(163, DisplayMode.ITEM, "Eat well. ", [ new KnowledgeNode(167, DisplayMode.POP, "Many studies such as this one have identified links between nutrition and cognitive performance."), new KnowledgeNode(168, DisplayMode.POP, "Results from Omlenet suggest that obtaining the recommended amounts of nutrients is very difficult."), ]), new KnowledgeNode(164, DisplayMode.ITEM, "Exercise often. ", [ new KnowledgeNode(166, DisplayMode.POP, "Check medical science articles") ]), new KnowledgeNode(165, DisplayMode.ITEM, "Maintain good posture. This includes not only how you hold your head or how high to have your monitors, but also the angle of your wrist when you use a mouse, keyboard, or phone."), //TODO: flesh out the individual considerations (maybe include a picture) ]), ]), makeTopLevelCategoryNode("Teamwork", [ new KnowledgeNode(116, DisplayMode.ITEM, "If your manager doesn't value the team's input, make them think it was their idea by asking them questions leading to it."), //Needs examples new KnowledgeNode(117, DisplayMode.ITEM, "Customers, stakeholders, and managers assign different weights to their values (such as cost and efficiency); you may have to search for benefits to your idea from different perspectives in order for it to be accepted."), //Needs examples new KnowledgeNode(160, DisplayMode.ITEM, "When reporting a problem, include multiple potential solutions."), new KnowledgeNode(171, DisplayMode.ITEM, "Avoid the bystander effect by making improvement your responsibility. ", [ new KnowledgeNode(172, DisplayMode.POP, "The bystander effect can be described as the inverse relationship between the number of people who are present and the number of people who take action. (Note: The Wikipedia article currently offers invalid counter-evidence by comparing absolute number of people who help in one study to the proportion of people who help in other studies. There may also be a number of people at which the effect is maximized.)"), new KnowledgeNode(173, DisplayMode.POP, "When you join a new team, document any misunderstandings and things you wish you knew from the start in order to optimize future onboarding."), new KnowledgeNode(174, DisplayMode.POP, "Ensure the existence of brief, overview-level documentation."), ]), ]), ``` Every response you provide must be strictly JavaScript code. Do not discuss before or after the code, but you should reason by writing comments before each node. Given all of this information, but without repeating any of the above, create a tree of information-dense KnowledgeNodes based on the following topic:

Dogmatism means you see concepts as rules to be followed without context. A pragmatist considers the pros and cons of each approach in-context and seeks the best answer for each specific situation.
Maintaining a healthy body and mind is paramount to being a good developer.
Even jet lag can harm your cognitive performance.
The amount and quality of your sleep affects your ability to think and your attitude.
Many studies such as this one have identified links between nutrition and cognitive performance.
Results from Omlenet suggest that obtaining the recommended amounts of nutrients is very difficult.
Maintain good posture. This includes not only how you hold your head or how high to have your monitors, but also the angle of your wrist when you use a mouse, keyboard, or phone.
